critique.summary: Meta announced that starting in 2026, Horizon Worlds and related VR experiences will be discontinued on Meta Quest headsets, shifting the platform to a mobile-only experience. Key features like Horizon Worlds, Events, and Horizon Hyperscape Capture will be removed or limited on VR devices by mid-2026, with users encouraged to transition to the mobile app for continued access.
mainPoints: Horizon Worlds and Events will be removed from the Meta Quest Store by March 31, 2026, and the app will be fully removed from Quest devices by June 15, 2026.
Certain popular worlds such as Horizon Central, Events Arena, Kaiju, and Bobber Bay will no longer be available in VR after March 31, 2026.
Horizon Hyperscape Capture (Beta) will no longer support sharing or co-experiencing captures after March 24, 2026, but existing captures remain viewable.
Horizon-specific perks in the Meta Horizon Plus subscription will be removed by March 31, 2026, though core gaming benefits remain unaffected.
Meta is focusing on improving Quest with new features and a modernized interface while separating VR and Horizon Worlds platforms.
critique.truthfulness: The claims in the article align with official announcements from Meta regarding the discontinuation of Horizon Worlds on Meta Quest VR headsets and the shift to a mobile-only experience. The timeline and details about feature removals and app availability are consistent with Meta's public communications. User comments reflect genuine community reactions but do not contradict the factual content of the announcement. No contradictory evidence was found in external sources.
considerations: The article does not discuss the potential impact on the user community, especially those who rely on VR for social interaction and content creation.
There is no mention of alternative platforms or migration paths for creators and users who want to preserve or transfer their VR content.
The technical and performance issues users experience with the mobile version of Horizon Worlds are noted in comments but not addressed by Meta in the announcement.
The article does not provide data or rationale from Meta explaining the business or strategic reasons behind this shift.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on Meta's discontinuation of Horizon Worlds on Meta Quest, with commenters expressing disappointment over the company's strategic missteps, perceived mismanagement, and wasted investment. Many highlight the challenges Meta faces in VR and the broader metaverse vision, while some speculate on layoffs and the future of Meta Quest hardware.

comments.author: atrus
comments.text:
Despite the shock at the amount of money meta spent on their version of the metaverse, I don't think they spent nearly enough to accomplish their vision.

Meta, to the detriment of the market, tried too early in the VR lifecycle to own the market. They basically tried to become the iPhone and Apple in the year 1990.

Tell me, do you believe any singular company in the year 1990, with 100B to burn, would be able to create the iPhone, in any of its varations? Absolutely not, there too much research, too much to invent, too much to program and not nearly enough talent and money for one company to manage.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a nuanced perspective on Meta's timing and investment scale, accurately reflecting the immense challenges in pioneering VR and metaverse technologies.

comments.author: tyleo
comments.text:
Interesting, cutting way back in the product they renamed the whole company for.

They feel a bit directionless to me. They are still making money but even their AI attempt feels half hearted. I think they are really trying but I’m not sure they can build the engineering muscle to move in new areas with the brand damage they’ve sustained.
comments.analysis: The comment insightfully critiques Meta's strategic and brand challenges, which align with public perceptions of the company's recent struggles.

comments.author: paxys
comments.text: It’s wild how much mismanagement Zuck is able to get away with just because he controls majority voting shares in Meta. At this point all of the missteps by the company in AR/VR, AI and everything else can directly be traced back to him. Half-baked vision, massive spending and no accountability. The company desperately needs a Sundar/Satya type leader.
comments.analysis: While somewhat emotional, this comment correctly identifies leadership issues as a factor in Meta's strategic missteps, a common critique in tech industry analysis.

comments.author: t-writescode
comments.text:
They were trying to compete with an existing, VERY good couple of alternatives, and the people most actually likely to use that product were already on those services.

It was a losing play that didn’t know what market it was actually entering.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately points out the competitive landscape and market misalignment that likely contributed to Horizon Worlds' failure.

comments.author: neosat
comments.text:
The sign of a company in absolute decline is when the worst possible place to get info about it, is their official announcement. Whoever wrote the announcement went out of their way to obfuscate the crux of the announcement (compare the clear heading on hackernews to their own heading)

If your (well paid) job is to write and communicate clearly, and for a major announcement you come up with this...not much left to say.
comments.analysis: The critique of Meta's communication is valid and reflects common frustration with corporate messaging, though it is more subjective and less about factual accuracy.

comments.author: xd1936
comments.text: Unbelievable. They re-architected the whole operating system around this stupid app. They discontinued their previous homescreen environments in favor of trying to promote Horizon Worlds, only to discontinue the blasted thing anyway? After all of those millions of dollars spent trying to make virtual events happen?
comments.analysis: This comment highlights a plausible misallocation of resources and strategic reversal, though it lacks detailed evidence.

comments.author: drivebyhooting
comments.text: When will horizon worlds be axed entirely? It has something like fewer than 10,000 active users at any given time.
comments.analysis: While the exact user count may vary, this aligns with reported low engagement metrics for Horizon Worlds.

comments.author: cheriot
comments.text:
Condolences to those that about to be laid off

Context: https://techcrunch.com/2026/03/14/meta-reportedly-considerin...
comments.analysis: This comment reflects reported news about layoffs, which is consistent with external sources.

comments.author: jesse_dot_id
comments.text:
I never understood why they were trying to recreate real life social interactions in VR, because it's worse by default, and the majority of the nerds who buy this tech are probably trying to escape that on some level. I know that any time I went into Meta Horizon Worlds, I didn't want to hear 95% of the people I heard talking.

What I do use VR for is Bigscreen VR nearly every night to watch stuff with my friends. Scrolling through reels in a movie theater is pretty fun and even though I never do it solo on my phone, I will sit there for like 3-4 hours in VR enjoying communal brain rot.

Perhaps they should focus on things like that instead of gimmicks that nobody cares about. For example, I have never once played a game in VR that didn't force me to sit or stand in a specific position, meaning to play it, I have to go out of my way to do so.
comments.analysis: This personal usage insight highlights a common critique of VR social apps and suggests alternative successful VR use cases.

comments.author: ralusek
comments.text: Maybe I'm just naive but I don't really understand discontinuing things like this. Like, unless there are like 100 people using this, how can it not be possible to just leave this running at like 0.5% of its former capacity. Just leave up like 1 server, collapse all of the DBs into one, and let these few autists have their stuff.
comments.analysis: This comment raises a reasonable operational question about discontinuation versus minimal maintenance, reflecting common user concerns.
